coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dot-based hydrogels for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Ibarbia, Antton
Sánchez-Abella, Laura
Lezama, Luis
Grande, Hans J.
Ruiz, Virginia
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQD) covalently immobilized in hydrogels have shown great promise as artificial enzymes for the photodegradation of the organic dye rhodamine B (RhB). Suitably functionalized with cross-linkable 3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate (MPS) units, GOQD-MPS were incorporated by free radical copolymerization into poly[2-(Methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium]-co-(3-Sulfopropylmethacrylate) 50:50 (A50coS50) hydrogels with known antiadhesive properties. The peroxidase mimetic activity of GOQD solutions for the oxidation of chromogenic peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in presence of hydrogen peroxide increased significantly upon functionalization with MPS. Consequently, composite GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels also exhibited high peroxidase activity for TMB oxidation. Moreover, GOQD-MPS solutions and GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels showed remarkable catalytic activity for degradation of model RhB dye in darkness, an activity that was notably enhanced by visible light irradiation. The photoactivity depended on GOQD-MPS loading and hydrogel volume. Interestingly, GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels retained the high photocatalytic activity after several months stored wet as well as after undergoing drying-rehydration processes, key from a practical point of view for applications in water treatment. The activity of rehydrated hydrogels for RhB photodegradation was only reduced by 15% compared to the activity of a non-dried hydrogel under the same test conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectroscopy (A) and TGA under air atmosphere (B) of pristine GOQD, MPS and GOQD-MPS.

(A) Schematic representation of the synthesis of GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels by free radical thermal polymerization. Photographs of representative samples of neat A50coS50 hydrogel (B) and GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels with 1% (C), 2.5% (D) and 5% (E) GOQD-MPS loadings.

(A) FTIR spectra, (B) chemical composition (in % at.) by EDX analysis, (C) UV–Visible absorption spectra and (D) XRD patterns for GOQD-MPS, neat A50coS50 hydrogel and GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels with different GOQD-MPS content.
(A) SEM micrographs of freeze-dried A50coS50 hydrogels. (B) Equilibrium swelling ratio of neat A50coS50 and GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels.

Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm for 0.5 mM TMB solutions containing 40 μg/mL GOQD (A) or GOQD-MPS (B) and different concentrations of H2O2: (A) 0 (dotted), 0.1, 0.13, 0.2, 1, 2, 5, 10 and 50 mM; (B) 0 (dotted), 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.13, 0.2, 0.5 and 1 mM. Insets: photographs of the reaction mixtures with 1 mM H2O2 after 600 s.

(A) Time-dependent absorbance changes at 652 nm for 0.5 mM TMB solutions with 1 mM (circles) and 10 mM (diamonds) H2O2 in contact with a 1% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel. Control test without hydrogel in 0.5 mM TMB and 1 mM H2O2 solution (squares). Inset: photographs of reaction mixtures after 1800 s. (B) Absorbance at 652 nm of 0.5 mM TMB and 1 mM H2O2 solutions after 600 s in contact with (from left to right): 1% GOQD- A50coS50 hydrogel, dissolved GOQD-MPS or GOQD (40 μg/mL), a neat A50coS50 hydrogel and 40 μg/mL MPS.

Degradation of 10 μg/mL RhB in presence of 1 mM H2O2 by 100 μg/mL GOQD (A, B) or GOQD-MPS (C, D) under dark and visible light conditions. RhB absorption spectra (solid lines) at different exposure times (0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 h) with GOQD (A) and GOQD-MPS (C), with spectra of 100 μg/mL GOQD or GOQD-MPS alone (dotted lines). Evolution of relative RhB concentration with time in dark and illuminated conditions (D/L) without catalyst (control) and with GOQD (B) or GOQD-MPS (D).

Effect of the OH radical scavenger TBA (100 μg/mL) in the degradation of 10 μg/mL RhB in 1 mM H2O2 by 100 μg/mL GOQD or GOQD-MPS after 6 h under dark and visible light conditions. (B) PL increment at 425 nm of 0.5 mM TA and 1 mM H2O2 solutions 30 min after addition of 100 μg/mL GOQD or GOQD-MPS. As a control, ΔPL for the catalyst-free solution. Excitation wavelength: 320 nm. (C) EPR spectra of DMPO-OH spin adduct taken after 30 min from a reaction mixture containing 10 and 20 μg/mL of GOQD-MPS with 50 mM DMPO and 200 mM H2O2 in PBS (pH 7.2).

(A) Degradation of 10 μg/mL RhB in presence of 1 mM H2O2 by 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 and neat A50coS50 hydrogels under dark and visible light conditions (D/L); photographs taken after 6 h. (B) RhB degradation after 6 h irradiation by GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels with different GOQD loadings. The effect of triplicating hydrogel volume at a same GOQD loading is also shown. (C) Photographs of a 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel fresh, dried and rehydrated.

(A) HPLC chromatograms of the N-de-ethylated intermediates measured at 500 nm at different irradiation times (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 24 h) of 10 μg/mL RhB and 1 mM H2O2 solutions in contact with a 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel. Peaks: (a) RhB, (b) DER, (c) EER, (d) DR, (e) ER, (f) R. (B) Time evolution of peak areas in HPLC for RhB and main N-de-ethylated degradation intermediates recorded at 500 nm.

Comparison of the apparent kinetic parameters Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum reaction rate (Vmax) for peroxidase substrates reported in literature for CQD and GQD prepared from different precursors and synthetic routes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dot-based hydrogels for photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQD) covalently immobilized in hydrogels have shown great promise as artificial enzymes for the photodegradation of the organic dye rhodamine B (RhB). Suitably functionalized with cross-linkable 3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl methacrylate (MPS) units, GOQD-MPS were incorporated by free radical copolymerization into poly[2-(Methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium]-co-(3-Sulfopropylmethacrylate) 50:50 (A50coS50) hydrogels with known antiadhesive properties. The peroxidase mimetic activity of GOQD solutions for the oxidation of chromogenic peroxidase substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in presence of hydrogen peroxide increased significantly upon functionalization with MPS. Consequently, composite GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels also exhibited high peroxidase activity for TMB oxidation. Moreover, GOQD-MPS solutions and GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels showed remarkable catalytic activity for degradation of model RhB dye in darkness, an activity that was notably enhanced by visible light irradiation. The photoactivity depended on GOQD-MPS loading and hydrogel volume. Interestingly, GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels retained the high photocatalytic activity after several months stored wet as well as after undergoing drying-rehydration processes, key from a practical point of view for applications in water treatment. The activity of rehydrated hydrogels for RhB photodegradation was only reduced by 15% compared to the activity of a non-dried hydrogel under the same test conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQD) have attracted enormous interest in recent years due to their remarkable electronic and optical properties [1,2]. GQD are zero-dimensional graphene nanoparticles that retain the highly crystalline structure of single- or few-layered graphene. They are characterized by stable and easily tunable photoluminescence, high surface area and chemical stability. These features, along with their ease of synthesis and high biocompatibility, make GQD excellent candidates for applications in biosensing, drug delivery, bioimaging, opto-electronic devices, and catalysis [3–7].

Among all reported uses, a very promising feature of GQD is their potential to replace natural enzymes in practical applications. Even though these proteins possess high catalytic efficiency, specificity and selectivity, they suffer from drawbacks such as their cost and poor stability. Thus, the development of artificial enzymes (nanozymes) is highly desirable. In this regard, carbon nanomaterials have been used to effectively catalyze several important enzymatic reactions [8–10]. Specifically, GQD can be used to mimic horseradish peroxidase, an enzyme that catalyzes the oxidation of various organic compounds by H2O2 [11]. The peroxidase-like activity of GQD has been attributed to their ability to decompose H2O2 into hydroxyl (OH) radicals, which can subsequently oxidize different organic compounds. Many studies have exploited the peroxidase activity of GQD in different sensing assays for the detection of glucose [12–14], H2O2 [15,16], glutathione [17], uric acid [18], cholesterol [19] or ascorbic acid [20]. In contrast, there are comparably less examples for other important applications such as the degradation of toxic compounds like phenolic compounds [21] or methyl orange [22].

In parallel, considerable efforts have been made to decipher the mechanism and role of different functional groups of GQD in peroxidatic reactions [23–26]. Nearly all research on the peroxidase-like activity of GQD has been carried out in solution, which is essential from a fundamental perspective for a better understanding of the relationship between GQD composition and activity. However, from a more practical point of view, it is highly desirable to immobilize the active GQD in a supporting and protecting structure or scaffold. To the best of our knowledge, only one study [27] has tackled this issue, demonstrating the peroxidase-like activity of cotton fabric supported GQD, specifically, GQD impregnated in band-aids for wound disinfection.

In this regard, hydrogels represent very promising immobilization supports for enzymes, providing them with an aqueous microenvironment that preserve their activity [28–30]. As a typical 3D macrostructure, hydrogels consist of a network of crosslinked hydrophilic polymer chains that do not dissolve in water. Reactants can diffuse freely in the swollen network of hydrogels. Carbon-based nanozymes such as graphene oxide have been immobilized in hydrogels, showing high stability and activity for enzymatic-like catalysis in organic solvents [31]. In the specific case of GQD, different GQD-based composite hydrogels have been proposed for a number of applications such as heavy metal scavenging [32], colorimetric or fluorescent sensing platforms [33,34], lubricating coatings [35], drug delivery systems [36,37], LEDs [38] or simply to impart the hydrogel with enhanced mechanical properties [39,40]. However, to the best of our knowledge, GQD composite hydrogels with enzymatic peroxidase-like activity have not been reported yet.

In this work, GOQD functionalized with MPS were covalently incorporated into poly[2-(Methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium]-co-(3-Sulfopropylmethacrylate) (50:50) (A50coS50) hydrogels prepared via free radical copolymerization, in order to avoid GOQD lixiviates even in harsh conditions. A50coS50 hydrogels are known to have antiadhesive properties [41] derived from their pseudo-zwitterionic nature due to their positive and negative mixed charged moieties. The resulting GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels exhibit remarkable peroxidase mimetic activity, as evidenced by the catalytic oxidation of the peroxidase substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2. The composite hydrogels also show high capability to degrade RhB dye as model of organic pollutant [42]. Moreover, degradation activity of the hydrogels remained stable after several months stored wet and after being dried and rehydrated.

Starting materials were purchased from the following commercial suppliers: Vulcan® XC-72 carbon black from Cabot Corporation; 3-sulfopropyl methacrylate potassium salt (SPMA), [2-(methacryloyloxy)ethyl]trimethylammonium chloride solution (75% w/w in H2O) (TMAEMA), poly(ethylene glycol) dimethacrylate with a molecular weight of 550 Da (PEGDMA550), MPS, hydrogen peroxide (30% w/w), TMB, tert-Butyl alcohol, potassium persulfate (KPS) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) from Sigma Aldrich; nitric acid (65% w/w), sodium acetate anhydrous, phosphate buffer saline (0.05 M) pH 7.2 (PBS) and acetic acid (96% w/w) from Scharlab; terephthalic acid (TA) and RhB from Acros Organics. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of Synthesis A10 from Millipore (18 Ω.cm).

GOQD were synthesized by oxidative exfoliation of carbon black particles adapting a method reported elsewhere [43]. Typically, Vulcan® XC-72 carbon black (2 g) was refluxed in 250 mL of 6 M HNO3 for 72 h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension was centrifuged (3500 rpm) for 60 min to obtain a supernatant and a sediment. The supernatant was heated to evaporate the water and nitric acid and a reddish-brown solid (GOQD) was obtained. GOQD were redispersed in ultrapure water and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 24 h. Water was finally removed by rotary evaporation.

GOQD were functionalized with MPS to be used as crosslinkers in GQD-A50coS50 hydrogels. In a typical procedure, GOQD and MPS (1:2 ratio) were dissolved in ultrapure water and stirred for 24 h at room temperature. GOQD-MPS were dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3500 Da) for 24 h to remove unreacted MPS. The solution was finally freeze dried to obtain purified GOQD-MPS.

GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels with different GOQD-MPS content were prepared using stock solutions (20 mg/mL) of GOQD-MPS in PBS. The hydrogels were prepared by thermal-initiated free radical copolymerization of TMAEMA and SPMA comonomers with the bifunctional comonomer PEGDMA550 as cross-linker in presence of KPS as initiator. A typical procedure for the preparation of 1 mL of 5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels is as follows: SPMA (43 mg, 0.17 mmol), TMAEMA (48 μL, 36 mg, 0.17 mmol) and PEGDMA550 (19 μL, 21 mg, 0.04 mmol), were added to a vial containing 250 μL of the stock solution of GOQD-MPS and 600 μL of PBS. The mixture was degassed by bubbling nitrogenthrough the system for at least 10 min.Then, 40 μL of KPS solution (50 mg/mL) was added and mixed vigorously for 10 s. Four eppendorfs were filled with 250 μL of the polymerization mixture and placed in an oven at 70 °C for 2 h. For the preparation of 1 mL of 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50, 100 μL of the stock solution of GOQD-MPS and 750 μL of PBS was used instead. In turn, for the preparation of 1 mL of 1% GOQD-A50coS50, 50 μL of the stock solution of GOQD-MPS and 800 μL of PBS was used. For the preparation of bare A50coS50, 850 μL of PBS was used. In this way, the solid content of the polymerization mixture was maintained. The resulting hydrogels were removed from the molds and placed in DI water at ambient temperature for 3 days, changing the water once per day to remove any unreacted monomers, initiator, uncrosslinked polymers, etc. The gel fraction was calculated using the following equation:
Gel
fraction =
W dry
W theory
x 100
where Wdry is the weight of the hydrogel after extraction from water and freeze-dried and Wtheory is the theoretical weight considering 100% of monomer conversion.

The washing supernatants were analyzed by UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy at a characteristic wavelength of GOQD-MPS aqueous solutions (350 nm) to quantify the amount of GOQD-MPS not anchored to the A50coS50 hydrogels.

Fourier-transformed infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of GOQD and freeze-dried hydrogels was performed at room temperature using a Jasco 4100LE spectrophotometer with 4 cm−1 resolution in a wavenumber range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of GOQD was performed with a TGA Q500 (TA instruments) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 under air atmosphere. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of freeze-dried hydrogels were recorded between 10 and 100° (2θ) with 2 s/0.02° step size on a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer with a Cu-Kα source (λ = 0.154 nm) and equipped with a LynxEye PSD detector. UV–Visible absorption spectra of GOQD solutions and hydrated hydrogels were measured with a Jasco V-570 UV/VIS/NIR spectrophotometer in quartz cuvettes of 10 and 1 mm optical path lengths, respectively. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQD solutions and hydrated hydrogels were measured using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer in quartz cuvettes of 10 and 1 mm optical path lengths, respectively. The microstructure and chemical composition of freeze-dried hydrogels was examined by a JEOL JSM 7600F scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with energy dispersive X-Ray (EDX) spectrometer. Fully swollen hydrogels deposited on carbon tape were freeze-dried, sputter-coated with a thin layer of gold and analyzed by SEM using an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Freeze-dried hydrogels were characterized by bright field Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) with a TitanG2 60–300 TEM at 300 kV accelerating voltage. In order to minimize charging and decrease corresponding damage of the hydrogel a 100 μm objective aperture was inserted. Samples were prepared by crashing the dry hydrogel in liquid nitrogen, warming the sample in a flow of nitrogen to prevent wetting and dry depositing the powder on a lacey TEM grid. Imaging was made at low dose rate, and multiple images of the same place were aligned and averaged, which resulted in a final resolution of ~3 Å at reasonable signal-to-noise ratio besides notable movement and shrinkage of the hydrogel. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements were performed at room temperature using a Bruker ELEXSYS E500 spectrometer operating at the X-band. The spectrometer was equipped with a super-high-Q resonator ER-4123-SHQ. Samples were placed into quartz glass capillary tubes with 0.6 mm inner diameter. Typical instrument settings were: centre field, 3345 G; scan range, 100 G; receiver gain, 39 dB; time constant, 81.92 ms; modulation amplitude, 1.0 G; microwave power, 2 mW. Data was collected and processed using the Bruker Xepr suite. Intermediate products of RhB degradation were investigated by HPLC (Agilent 1100) equipped with a UV–Visible absorption diode array detector using a Zorbax Agilent C18 column (150 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm) held at 30 °C and eluted with a gradient solvent from 60% to 90% of MeOH in water containing 0.1% of trifluoroacetic acid at a flow rate of 1 mL/min. Compounds were detected at a wavelength of 550 nm.

The swelling ratios of the hydrogels were determined by gravimetric method using the equation:
Equilibrium
Swelling
Ratio =
W swell
-
W dry
W dry
where Wswell is the weight of the fully swollen hydrogel (equilibrated hydrogel after the purification step) and Wdry the weight after freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase activity and RhB degradation assays
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation of TMB in presence of H2O2 was measured to assess the peroxidase mimetic activity of GOQD and GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels. GOQD or GOQD-MPS (40 μg/mL) were dispersed in 0.1 M acetate buffer solutions (pH 3.6) containing 0.5 mM TMB and different concentrations (0.01–50 mM) of H2O2. Likewise, GOQD-A50coS50 and GOQD-free hydrogel samples (200 μL) were tested in 5 mL acetate buffer solutions with the same TMB and H2O2 concentrations as in GOQD solutions. Control experiments without H2O2 and GOQD were also performed. The optical absorption of the solutions was measured at regular time intervals in a UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometer. Measurements were replicated three times.

RhB degradation catalyzed by dispersed GOQD, GOQD-MPS or GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels in presence of H2O2 both in dark and under visible light illumination was also followed spectrophotometrically at regular time intervals. Samples were irradiated by compact fluorescent lamp (22 W, 1000 lm) at 5 cm distance. Absorbance evolution of aqueous solutions (5 mL) containing 10 μg/mL RhB, 100 μg/mL GOQD (or GOQD-MPS) and 1 mM H2O2 stored in dark and illuminated were compared. In the case of GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels, the activity of hydrogel samples prepared from 200 μL stock solutions was tested in aqueous solutions (5 mL) containing the same concentration of RhB (10 μg/mL) and H2O2 (1 mM) as in tests with dispersed GOQD and GOQD-MPS. Three replicates for each condition (dark and illuminated) were measured together with control experiments of RhB solutions without GOQD, H2O2 or GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels.

For TA assays, 3 mL solutions containing 1 mM H2O2 and 0.5 mM TA, with and without 100 μg/mL GOQD (or GOQD-MPS) were subjected to visible light irradiation under the same conditions as in RhB degradation reactions. The generation of fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TA-OH), with a characteristic emission at 425 nm, was followed by PL measurements of the solutions after 30 min. For EPR measurements, samples contained 50 mM DMPO, 200 mM H2O2 and 10–20 mg/L GOQS-MPS in 0.05 M phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.2). Spectra were recorded under dark and ambient light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GOQD and GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel preparation and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: As described in the experimental section, GOQD were synthesized by oxidative exfoliation of carbon black particles, an attractive synthetic route due to its simplicity, scalability, high yield and use of an inexpensive precursor. A detailed characterization of GOQD prepared under identical conditions was reported in a previous work from our group [44]. Briefly, GOQD have an average lateral size of 2.8 ± 0.7 nm and a thickness of 1.0 ± 0.3, as determined by TEM (Fig. S1) and AFM, respectively. XPS characterization revealed that GOQD consist of 56.7% C, 37.0% O and 6.3% N, that is, they have high oxygen content as a result of the strong oxidative conditions used in the synthesis. In particular, the following functional groups were identified in GOQD by XPS and FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1 A) in addition to C–C bonds: OH (with C-OH and O–H vibrations at 1080 cm−1 and in the 3300–3500 cm−1 range), C-O-C, OC-OH and C=O groups with vibration bands at 1225, 1450 and 1705 cm−1, respectively. FTIR spectroscopy corroborated the successful functionalization of GOQD with MPS, as the spectrum of GOQD-MPS exhibits additional bands characteristic of the silane (Fig. 1A). The intense bands at 2973 and 2887 cm−1 can be assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations while bands at 1379 and 1450 cm−1 are related to C–H bending vibrations of –CH2- groups in the propyl chain of MPS. In addition, the strong absorption bands at 1718 and 1638 cm−1 in the spectrum of GOQD-MPS can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C=C bonds, respectively of the methacryloxy unit of MPS that is present in GOQD-MPS. The characteristic bands of the silane group can also be observed in the spectrum of GOQD-MPS in the range of 942–1161 cm−1 due to Si–O stretching vibrations and at 774 and 845 cm−1 related to Si–C stretching vibrations.

Thermogravimetry also reveals differences between GOQD and GOQD-MPS that confirm the successful silanization of GOQD (Fig. 1B). Thermal decomposition of MPS occurs below 170 °C (TGA derivative is shown in Fig. S2). At this temperature, GOQD shows an 8% weight loss due to decomposition of labile oxygen-containing functional groups that is completed at 210 °C, reaching a 19% weight loss. However, GOQD-MPS exhibit a higher weight loss at 170 °C (11.5%), due to contribution from decomposition of MPS covalently attached to GOQD. This first decomposition process is completed at higher temperatures in GOQD-MPS (300 °C) as a result of a thermal stabilization of labile groups by covalent bonding of MPS chains. Finally, GOQD sheets undergo a slower two-step full decomposition between 280 and 570 °C than GOQD-MPS, which fully degrades in one step between 300 and 520 °C.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , A50coS50 hydrogels were synthesized by thermal-initiated free radical polymerization. TMAEMA was copolymerized with SPMA using the same amount of moles of each comonomer to keep balanced the neat charge of the final polymer. PEGDMA550 was used as cross-linker in the copolymerization reaction to form the 3D polymeric network. The gel fraction obtained after cleaning the neat hydrogels by dialysis is quite high, around 88%, indicating generation of a high amount of radicals that participate in the copolymerization reaction in absence of oxygen, which would otherwise scavenge some of the radicals formed, lowering the gel fraction. GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels were produced in-situ using a stock solution of GOQD-MPS in PBS. PBS was needed instead of deionized water to avoid pH changes during the hydrogel formation and flocculation of GOQD. It was observed that the gel fraction decreased from 88% to around 81% when GOQD were introduced irrespective of the amount of GOQD used in the polymerization. That is, the presence of GOQD does not affect significantly hydrogel formation and the small difference could be attributed to experimental errors rather than a possible radical scavenging activity of GOQD or physical hindrance during polymerization. After the washing steps with deionized water, the amount of GOQD not linked to the polymeric network (trapped) was quantified by UV–Visible absorbance of the washing media. In all cases the amount of GOQD covalently attached was 80%, irrespective of the initial amount of GOQD-MPS used. Later on, GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels were kept in deionized water for almost 6 months and no further GOQD release was detected by UV–Visible absorbance measurements, demonstrating the stability of GOQD covalently bonded to the polymeric network.

Further evidence of GOQD-MPS incorporation in the composite hydrogels was obtained by FTIR spectroscopy, XPS/EDX spectroscopy and UV–Visible absorbance measurements (Fig. 3 ). FTIR spectra (Fig. 3A) show the characteristic bands of chemical bonds in freeze-dried GOQD-MPS, neat and GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels, specifically: O–H vibrations in the 3300–3600 cm−1 range, asymmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations at 2973 and 2887 cm−1, C=O stretching vibrations at 1718 cm−1, C=C stretching vibrations at 1638 cm−1 and C–H bending vibrations at 1450 and 1379 cm−1. Despite the presence of these coincident chemical bonds in GOQD-MPS, neat and composite hydrogels, their relative amount in each material is different, as revealed by the corresponding band intensity ratios obtained from the FTIR spectra. Thus, C=C/C=O and C–H/C=O band intensity ratios in neat A50coS50 hydrogel are 1.04 and 1.05 respectively, which are substantially lower than in GOQD-MPS, with ratios of 1.25 and 1.44 respectively (Fig. S3). In the case of composite hydrogels, band intensity ratio values increase with GOQD-MPS loading, from 1.06 and 1.07 in 1% GOQD-A50coS50 to 1.12 and 1.14 in 5% GOQD-A50coS50, demonstrating GOQD-MPS incorporation in the composite hydrogels.

The presence of increasing amounts of GOQD-MPS in GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels was also corroborated by EDX spectroscopy (Fig. 3B). The relative content of O/C in GOQD-MPS is much higher (1.23) than that in the neat hydrogel (0.64) and hence this ratio increases in the composite hydrogels with GOQD-MPS loading from 0.70 to 0.73 for 1% and 5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels, respectively. In addition, S content is also lower in the composite hydrogels (2.9–3.7% at.) than in the neat hydrogel (4.1% at.) due to the lack of S in GOQD-MPS. UV–Visible absorbance measurements of hydrated hydrogels further confirmed the presence of GOQD-MPS in the composite hydrogels (Fig. 3C). While the neat hydrogel has negligible absorbance in the Visible region, GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels exhibit a strong absorbance in the 350–700 nm region that increases with GOQD-MPS loading. This enhanced absorption is due to the contribution of GOQD-MPS, with their characteristic optical features corresponding to high-energy π-π* and lower-energy n-π* electronic transitions. In addition, the characteristic PL emission fingerprint of GOQD-MPS centered at 530 nm (λexc = 470 nm) was clearly resolved in the PL emission spectra of GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels (Fig. S4), which contrasts with the negligible emission of the neat hydrogel in the Visible region.

XRD patterns of freeze-dried neat and GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels are compared in Fig. 3D. All hydrogels have an amorphous structure, with broad peaks at 2θ = 17.8°, 31.7° and 41.5° corresponding to different amorphous phases. Incorporation of GOQD-MPS causes a substantial decrease of peak intensity at 17.8° and 41.5° together with a broadening and shifting of the predominant peak at 31.7° to lower diffraction angles (31.0°-30.2°) with increasing GOQD-MPS loading. This indicates increased chain separation in composite hydrogels by incorporation of GOQD-MPS as crosslinkers. Analogous peak width increase and shift to lower diffraction angles was noted in other composite hydrogels (graphene oxide/polyvinyl alcohol) upon increase of covalent cross-linking [45].

SEM characterization of freeze-dried neat and GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels (Fig. 4 A) did not reveal significant differences of morphology. All hydrogels exhibited similar microstructure and due to their small size, GOQD-MPS nanoadditives were not resolved even at the highest concentration used (5% wt.). In turn, TEM characterization of the composite hydrogel with the highest GOQD-MPS loading, 5% GOQD-A50coS50, shows nanoparticles with less than 5 nm size although with very low contrast (Fig. S5D). It is worth noting that attaining high contrast with carbon-based nanoparticles (GOQD-MPS) in a carbon-based matrix (hydrogel) is very challenging. However, large-size agglomerates of nanoparticles were not seen across the hydrogel (Fig. S5), indicating that GOQD-MPS are well dispersed in the hydrogel matrix.

On the other hand, the water content (g/g) of fully swollen hydrogels (Fig. 4B) showed a clear dependence on the amount of GOQD-MPS in the composite hydrogels. When low GOQD-MPS loadings were covalently attached to the polymeric network, an increase of swelling with respect to that of the neat (GOQD-free) hydrogel was observed. This effect of highly functionalized GOQD could be attributed to formation of a network of hydrogen bonds between their functional groups and water resulting in a hydrogel network with improved swelling capacity. However, further increase of GOQD-MPS loading in the composite hydrogels led to swelling decrease. This trend could be explained by the fact that GOQD-MPS, which have more than one functional group that can form covalently links with the hydrogel polymeric network, can act as cross-linkers. At high GOQD-MPS loadings, the amount of cross-linkers and hence the density of the polymeric network increased, reducing swellability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase-like catalytic activity of GOQD and GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase mimetic activity of GOQD was evaluated with the catalytic oxidation of the chromogenic peroxidase substrate TMB in the presence of H2O2. The oxidation product of TMB in 0.1 M acetate buffer solutions (pH 3.6) has a characteristic blue color, with a prominent absorption band centered at 652 nm, and hence, the catalytic reaction can be followed spectrophotometrically. Fig. 5 A shows the evolution of absorbance at 652 nm with time for 0.5 mM TMB solutions containing 40 μg/mL GOQD and different concentrations (0.1–50 mM) of H2O2. As can be seen, absorbance increased with increasing H2O2 concentration in presence of GOQD whereas color change was negligible in control experiments without GOQD or H2O2 in the same timescale, demonstrating the intrinsic peroxidase-like activity of GOQD. It is worth pointing out that absorbance of 40 μg/mL GOQD solution at this wavelength is negligible (0.010). In related materials, such as graphene oxide and carbon nanodots, this catalytic activity has been attributed to electron transfer from lone-pair electrons in TMB amino groups to the carbon catalyst with subsequent electron transfer from the carbon catalyst to H2O2, resulting in faster TMB oxidation rates by H2O2 [12].

As indicated in the introduction, several GQD with peroxidase mimetic activity have been reported recently, although reaction kinetics differs considerably depending on the synthetic method. Kinetic parameters can be obtained from absorbance measurements by changing one substrate concentration (TMB or H2O2) and fixing that of the other substrate. Concentration of oxidized TMB is determined from the absorbance using Beer-Lambert law (using a molar absorption coefficient of oxidized TMB, ε = 39000 M−1 cm−1). Then, initial reaction rates (V0) at different substrate concentrations ([S]) are calculated from the slopes of the concentration vs. time linear plots. With these values, the apparent Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximum reaction rate (Vmax) can be obtained from the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation: 1/V0 = Km/Vmax ⋅1/[S] + 1/Vmax. Lineweaver-Burk plots for GOQD-catalyzed TMB oxidation keeping TMB concentration constant (0.5 mM) and varying H2O2 concentration in the range 0.1–50 mM are shown in Fig. S6A. From the slope and intercept of the linear regression, a Km value of 0.61 mM and Vmax of 6.8 × 10−9 M⋅s−1 were obtained for GOQD. Km is a measure of the binding affinity between enzyme and substrate (the smaller Km, the stronger the affinity), while high Vmax values indicate high catalytic activity.

Table 1 summarizes the apparent kinetic parameters for peroxidase substrates reported in literature for GQD prepared from different precursors and synthetic routes. As can be seen, very broad value ranges have been reported for Km (0.012–2.3 mM) and Vmax (1.4 × 10−9 − 4.6 × 10−6 M⋅s−1), highlighting enormous differences in the peroxidase activity of GQD with different composition. Compared to other GQD, GOQD obtained here from thermal acid treatment of carbon black particles exhibit moderate binding affinity (Km = 0.61 mM) and modest catalytic activity (Vmax = 6.8 × 10−9 M⋅s−1), which is comparable to that of GQD prepared also by thermal acid treatment but using graphene or graphene oxide as precursors [16,23].

Next, the peroxidase mimetic activity of GOQD functionalized with MPS was also examined prior to be incorporated as crosslinkers in hydrogels to ensure that functionalization did not suppress or reduce significantly their catalytic activity. Fig. 5B shows the evolution of absorbance at 652 nm with time for 0.5 mM TMB solutions containing 40 μg/mL GOQD-MPS and different concentrations (0.01–1 mM) of H2O2. As in the case of pristine GOQD, absorbance increased with increasing H2O2 concentration in presence of GOQD-MPS. Absorbance of 40 μg/mL GOQD-MPS solutions at 652 nm is negligible (0.016) as well as color change in control experiments in the same timescale with 0.5 mM TMB without addition of GOQD-MPS or H2O2, revealing the catalytic role of GOQD-MPS. Interestingly, color changes associated to the product of TMB oxidation were more pronounced with GOQD-MPS (>10-fold) than with pristine GOQD at a same H2O2 concentration, indicating a higher peroxidase-like activity of GOQD-MPS. The differences are illustrated in the photographs shown in Fig. 5 of cuvettes with reaction mixtures containing the same concentration of TMB, H2O2 (1 mM) and GOQD (Fig. 5A) or GOQD-MPS (Fig. 5B) at 600 s.

The apparent kinetic parameters for TMB oxidation catalyzed by GOQD-MPS were also determined from Lineweaver–Burk linear plots where V0 values at different H2O2 concentrations were estimated from absorbance measurements keeping TMB concentration constant at 0.5 mM. From the slope and intercept of the linear regression (Fig. S6B), a Km of 1.7 mM and Vmax of 7.5 × 10−7 M⋅s−1 were obtained for GOQD-MPS (Table 1), values that indicate a decrease in binding affinity and a remarkable increase in catalytic activity of GOQD upon functionalization. Several studies have reported the effect of GQD surface modification in their peroxidase activity [23,25,26]. In a seminal work, Sun et al. identified the catalytically active and substrate-binding sites in GQD by selectively deactivating different functional groups and evaluating the effect on their intrinsic peroxidase activity [23]. Supported by experimental evidence and theory, they concluded that hydroxyl groups inhibited GQD peroxidase activity. In line with these results, we attribute the enhanced activity of GOQD-MPS to a lower density of hydroxyl groups as a result of the silanization reaction with MPS. In addition, steric hindrance caused by covalently grafted MPS chains may explain the higher Km value due to less accessible substrate-binding sites.

Finally, the peroxidase activity of A50coS50 composite hydrogels with GOQD-MPS as crosslinkers was also assessed with TMB as substrate in acetate buffer solutions (pH 3.6) with the same TMB and H2O2 concentrations as those used in tests for dispersed GOQD. Hydrogel samples (prepared from 250 μL stock solutions) were immersed in 5 mL volume reaction mixtures in 10 mL vials, which were placed in an orbital shaker and the solution absorbance was measured at regular time intervals. Fig. 6 A shows the evolution of absorbance at 652 nm with time of TMB solutions in contact with 1% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels at two different H2O2 concentrations (1 and 10 mM). As can be seen, the amount of oxidized TMB increases with time and H2O2 concentration in solutions containing the hydrogels, in clear contrast with control experiments without hydrogel where negligible color changes were noted (photographs in Fig. 6A). The results indicate that GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels exhibit notable peroxidase mimetic activity. That is, A50coS50 hydrogel behaves as a promising immobilization support for GOQD-MPS, with the hydrophilic polymer chains swelling in water and allowing both reactants and products to diffuse and react with GOQD-based nanozymes.

With the aim of comparing qualitatively the peroxidase activity of hydrogel-supported and free (dissolved) GOQD-MPS, Fig. 6B shows absorbance values at 600 s of identical TMB and H2O2 solutions with a same concentration (40 μg/mL) of GOQD-based nanozymes, either immobilized in the hydrogel or dissolved in the reaction mixture. The yield of the reaction catalyzed by the GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogel after 600 s is about 10 times lower than that of free GOQD-MPS but 3 times higher than that of free pristine GOQD. That is, functionalized GOQD in the hydrogel still retain higher activity than free GOQD but diffusion of reactants and products to the catalytic sites inside the hydrogel slows down the catalytic reaction. In fact, absorbance of the reaction solution with GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels continues to increase linearly after 1800 s (Fig. 6A) whereas in the case of free GOQD-MPS, it reaches a plateau already at 600 s (Fig. 5B). Moreover, the catalytic activity of the hydrogels may be underestimated as a result of adsorption of the oxidized product of TMB by the hydrogels. On the other hand, negligible absorbance of control solutions with the neat hydrogel or dissolved MPS (Fig. 6B) in the same timescale indicates that none of them are responsible for the activity of GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels. After proving the peroxidase activity of free and hydrogel-encapsulated GOQD-MPS with peroxidase substrate TMB, the activity of these enzymatic systems to degrade organic dyes in presence of H2O2 was further assessed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rhodamine B degradation activity of GOQD and GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: As OH radicals formed by peroxidase-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2 are known to be very active in the degradation of organic dyes, the activity of both peroxidase mimetic carbocatalysts, GOQD or GOQD-MPS was investigated by studying degradation of model dye RhB solutions in the presence of H2O2. Degradation was followed by measuring changes in the absorption spectrum of RhB solutions with reaction time for reaction mixtures (10 μg/mL RhB, 100 μg/mL GOQD or GOQD-MPS and 1 mM H2O2) stored in the dark and irradiated by visible light (Fig. 7 ). Control experiments were also carried out with carbocatalyst-free RhB and H2O2 solutions under both illumination conditions.

As can be seen in Fig. 7A and 7C for irradiated solutions, the characteristic absorption band of RhB, centered at 554 nm, is gradually quenched with exposure time in presence of GOQD and GOQD-MPS. At the concentration used, absorbance of GOQD and GOQD-MPS at 554 nm is not negligible (spectra in dotted lines), hence its contribution was subtracted to evaluate RhB degradation rates. Time evolution of RhB concentration (C) with respect to the initial value (C0) in presence and absence of GOQD or GOQD-MPS can be compared in Fig. 7B and 7D, respectively. As can be seen, degradation of RhB in control experiments without the catalysts is relatively small, specially under dark conditions (only 4% RhB degraded after 6 h) and somewhat higher under illumination (21% degraded after 6 h). However, degradation increases significantly with GOQD in dark (17% degraded after 6 h) and more remarkably when the solution was exposed to light (62%). That is, GOQD exhibit a modest peroxidase-like activity in the darkness, as revealed by TMB oxidation assays, but this catalytic activity is notably enhanced by visible light. A similar effect of visible light enhancing the catalytic activity of C-dots for degradation of methyl orange was reported by Ming et al. [22], who attributed the photo-activity to C-dot catalyzed decomposition of H2O and H2O2 into OH radicals with high oxidation ability of the organic dye. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of GOQD is H2O2 concentration-dependent and thus increases considerably with H2O2 concentration, from 18% RhB degradation after 3 h exposure with 1 μM H2O2 to 98% after the same time with 1 M H2O2 (Supporting information, Fig. S7A).

On the other hand, GOQD-MPS exhibited more peroxidase-like activity than GOQD, with 45% RhB degraded after 6 h in dark and a remarkably higher photoactivity, with 92% RhB degraded after 6 h when the solution was exposed to visible light (Fig. 7D). Almost total RhB decoloration after 6 h with GOQD-MPS can be seen in photographs of reaction mixtures (Fig. 7C), where the remaining pale orange color corresponds to GOQD-MPS. These results are in agreement with the better peroxidase-like activity of functionalized GOQD for the TMB oxidation assay. Moreover, RhB degradation efficiency can be further increased with catalyst concentration under dark and irradiated conditions (Supporting information, Fig. S7B and S7C), evidencing the catalytic role of the carbocatalysts. RhB degradation after 6 h increased from 13 to 29% (in dark) and from 49% to 68% (with light) when GOQD concentration varied from 50 to 200 μg/mL (Fig. S7B). In the case of GOQD-MPS, higher RhB degradation efficiency was reached, with values after only 3 h increasing from 14% to 47% (in dark) and from 77% to 97% (with light) as GOQD-MPS concentration varied from 50 to 200 μg/mL (Fig. S7C).

In order to confirm that OH radicals generated from decomposition of H2O and H2O2 are the active species in the reaction, the degradation of RhB catalyzed by GOQD and GOQD-MPS was investigated in the presence of an OH radical scavenger, tert-Butyl alcohol (TBA). As can be seen in Fig. 8 A, RhB degradation was notably reduced in presence of 100 μg/mL TBA with both catalysts under dark and irradiated conditions, specifically, 14.8% in dark and 34.7% with light for GOQD and 38.6% in dark and 44.1% with light for GOQD-MPS. This decrease of catalytic activity in presence of TBA due to consumption of OH radicals by the scavenger highlights their vital role in the degradation of RhB. The involvement of OH radicals was further confirmed with an OH tracking probe TA, which reacts with OH to generate fluorescent TA-OH, with a characteristic emission at 425 nm (scheme in Fig. 8B). Thus, fluorescence spectra of 0.5 mM TA and 1 mM H2O2 solutions alone (as control) and with 100 μg/mL GOQD or GOQD-MPS were measured at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm after different reaction times. Fig. 8B compares the fluorescence enhancement at 425 nm (ΔPL) after 30 min reaction in control and GQD-containing mixtures. The higher ΔPL upon addition of GOQD and GOQD-MPS compared to that of control solutions indicates formation of more TA-OH by reaction of TA with OH radicals, originated from the GQD-catalyzed decomposition of H2O2. In addition, EPR measurements using DMPO as spin trap also confirmed generation of OH radicals in PBS pH 7.2 with H2O2 (blank solution) in presence of GOQD-MPS, even in dark conditions. As shown in Fig. 8C, the typical EPR spectrum of DMPO-OH spin adduct was measured immediately upon addition of GOQD-MPS, displaying four characteristic lines with 1:2:2:1 relative intensities (Spin Hamiltonian parameters: g = 2.0056; aN = 15.0 G; aH = 14.8 G). While no signal was noted for the blank solution, EPR signal intensity increased with GOQD-MPS concentration, indicating generation of OH radicals by decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by GOQD-MPS. For a given mixture composition, the intensity of the EPR signal corresponding to the DMPO-OH adduct also increased with time in darkness (Fig. S8A) and by exposure to light (Fig. S8B).

Finally, the capability of A50coS50 composite hydrogels with GOQD-MPS as crosslinkers to degrade 10 μg/mL RhB in 1 mM H2O2 was evaluated. Fig. 9 A compares activity of neat and 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel, which corresponds to 100 μg/mL of GOQD-MPS in the reaction volume, the same concentration used in free (dispersed) GOQD-MPS catalysts. Neat A50coS50 hydrogel and 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel exhibit identical trends under dark conditions, with 10% RhB degraded after 6 h, which is slightly higher than that of hydrogel-free RhB solutions (4%), indicating some dye adsorption in both types of hydrogels. When exposed to light, degradation after 6 h increases from 20% for solutions in contact with the neat hydrogel (which was the same value as that noted in hydrogel-free RhB solutions) to 50% for 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel, illustrating the activity of GOQD-MPS in the hydrogel. In line with TMB assays, the yield of the reaction catalyzed by GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels is lower than that of dispersed GOQD-MPS at the same concentration (92% in 6 h), most likely due to slowed reaction rate caused by the diffusion of reactants in the hydrogel. Full RhB degradation with the 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogel was achieved after 24 h exposure whereas only 34% degraded in the same time in solutions without hydrogel or in contact with the neat hydrogel.

Later on, the degradation capacity of GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels with different GOQD-MPS loading was explored as in the case of free dissolved GOQD-MPS, where activity increased with catalyst concentration (Fig. S7C). As shown in Fig. 9B, activity increases from neat to composite hydrogels with 2.5% GOQD-MPS but no further increase was noted for higher (5–10%) GOQD-MPS loadings. This trend might be attributed to the lower swellability measured for GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels with high (>2.5%) GOQD-MPS loadings (Fig. 4B). It is worth noting that RhB degradation yield after 6 h for 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels is only two times lower than that of the same concentration (100 μg/mL) of free GOQD-MPS, indicating high RhB accessibility to active catalytic sites in the hydrogel. The reaction yield could be further enhanced by increasing hydrogel volume in contact with solution. Thus, Fig. 9B illustrates the effect of triplicating the volume of 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels in a same reaction mixture, with the degradation yield after 6 h increasing from 50% (with 1 hydrogel) to 71% (with 3 hydrogels). In the control tests with 3 neat hydrogels RhB decoloration reached only 41%.

Additionally, the stability of hydrogels stored in ultrapure water as well as their activity after being dried and rehydrated was investigated, as it is crucial from a practical point of view. RhB degradation tests were carried out with 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels stored in ultrapure water for 3, 6 and 20 weeks under the same conditions as with fresh hydrogels. Interestingly, hydrogel activity did not decrease significantly with storage time, with RhB degradation yields after 6 h ranging from 50% for fresh to 40% (after 3 weeks), 43% (after 6 weeks) and 48% (after 20 weeks). In addition, 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels were dried in an oven at 50 °C and rehydrated in ultrapure water (photographs in Fig. 9C). The photocatalytic activity of rehydrated hydrogels for RhB degradation was not reduced substantially, only 15% compared to the activity of a non-dried hydrogel under the same test conditions. The excellent long-term stability of hydrogels stored in water as well as rehydrated after being stored dry with respect to their photocatalytic degradation activity of RhB makes GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels very promising materials for applications in dye degradation in water.

Finally, the mechanism of RhB photodegradation by GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels was explored by HPLC equipped with a UV–Vis diode array detector. Fig. 10 A shows HPLC chromatograms of 10 μg/mL RhB and 1 mM H2O2 solutions in contact with 2.5% GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels collected at different irradiation times. The peak labelled as a (tR = 7.2 min) that decreases with time corresponds to RhB. As photodegradation proceeds, five additional peaks labelled b to f (tR from 6.0 to 3.7 min) emerge, which can be assigned to the following N-de-ethylated degradation intermediates: (b) N,N-diethyl-N’-ethylrhodamine (DER), (c) N-ethyl-N’-ethylrhodamine (EER), (d) N,N-diethylrhodamine (DR), (e) N-ethylrhodamine (ER) and (f) rhodamine (R) [46]. The formation of a series of N-de-ethylated degradation intermediates during RhB photodegradation is also revealed by the hypsochromic shift of the absorption maximum (from 554 nm to 540 nm) of the irradiated solution (Fig. 7C). Fig. 10B shows variations of the two main N-de-ethylated intermediates from the degradation of RhB (DER and R) at different irradiation times given by the corresponding HPLC peak areas at 500 nm. As can be seen, the N-de-ethylated intermediates can be detected during the whole photodegradation process, confirming that RhB degradation by GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels occurred via the N-de-ethylation reaction pathway also reported for other photocatalysts [46,47].

We have proposed a method to produce GOQD-based hydrogels with high and stable photocatalytic activity for degradation of organic dyes. Specifically, GOQD functionalized with crosslinkable MPS units were incorporated by thermal-initiated free radical copolymerization as building blocks into A50coS50 hydrogels to produce GOQD-A50coS50 composite networks with different GOQD-MPS loadings. The crosslinking role of GOQD-MPS had an effect in the composite hydrogel swellability, which in turn affected the peroxidase-like enzymatic activity of the GOQD-A50coS50 composite hydrogels. Interestingly, GOQD functionalization with the crosslinkable MPS units led to an outstanding increase of the peroxidase catalytic activity of parent GOQD, assessed with the oxidation reaction of TMB in presence of H2O2. GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels also exhibited notable peroxidase mimetic activity, demonstrating that A50coS50 hydrogel behaves as a promising immobilization support for GOQD-MPS, with the hydrophilic polymer chains swelling in water and allowing both reactants and products to diffuse and react with GOQD-based nanozymes. The peroxidase enzymatic activity of these hybrid materials was exploited for the degradation of model dye RhB solutions in the presence of H2O2. Remarkably, the peroxidase-like activity of GOQD-based carbocatalysts in the darkness was notably enhanced by visible light. The use of radical scavengers and tracking probes revealed that OH radicals generated from decomposition of H2O and H2O2 were the active species in the degradation reaction. The photodegradation yield of RhB catalyzed by GOQD-A50coS50 hydrogels varied with GOQD loading and hydrogel volume. Moreover, the composite hydrogels retained the high photocatalytic activity after several months stored wet as well as after being dried and rehydrated, which makes them very promising materials in water treatment for degradation of organic pollutants.
